title: SEGMENT 12 — Plant-derived natural products: taxol, vincristine, and the opioids in yeast

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — Plant-derived natural products: taxol, vincristine, and the opioids in yeast
For most of human history, the most chemically interesting molecules we made and used as medicines came from plants.

The morphine in the poppy, the quinine in the cinchona bark, the digitoxin in the foxglove, the taxol in the Pacific yew, the vincristine in the rosy periwinkle — these are some of the most chemically complex molecules in the pharmacopoeia, and they are all evolved by plants for their own purposes, mostly defense against herbivores and pathogens.

The active molecules are typically present in tiny quantities in particular tissues, the plants grow slowly, the supplies are unstable, and total chemical synthesis — making the molecules from petrochemical starting materials — is often possible in principle but ruinously expensive in practice.
This is an obvious application area for metabolic engineering.
If you can find the biosynthetic pathway in the plant and install it in yeast, you can produce the drug in fermenters instead of in fields.
That promise has driven a huge fraction of the modern metabolic engineering research portfolio.
Let me give you three case studies.
Taxol.
Paclitaxel, sold as Taxol, is one of the most successful cancer drugs of the last fifty years, used against breast, ovarian, and lung cancers.
It is a diterpenoid extracted, originally, from the bark of the Pacific yew tree, Taxus brevifolia.
The original extraction process required killing the tree to harvest the bark, which was a problem because the yew is slow-growing and the supply could not possibly meet medical demand.

Eventually, a semi-synthetic process was developed that started from a precursor — 10-deacetylbaccatin III — that could be extracted, non-destructively, from the needles of the more abundant European yew, and converted chemically to paclitaxel.
That is the dominant process today.
But ongoing metabolic engineering work has aimed to produce paclitaxel — or at least the late-stage precursor — directly in engineered yeast or E. coli.
The pathway is long and complicated; it involves the cyclization of a diterpenoid called taxadiene followed by many oxidative and tailoring steps, mostly catalyzed by P450 enzymes.
Several groups have engineered E. coli and yeast to produce taxadiene at high levels.

The downstream tailoring steps remain harder, with not all of the P450s yet characterized, but the project is making steady progress, and a fully fermentative taxol pathway is broadly considered a matter of time and engineering, not a fundamental science problem.
Vincristine and vinblastine.
These are the vinca alkaloids, extracted from the rosy periwinkle, Catharanthus roseus, and used for various leukemias and lymphomas.
The biosynthesis is one of the most complex characterized in plants — over thirty enzymatic steps from a precursor amino acid to the final dimeric alkaloid.

The pathway was largely characterized in the 2010s by the work of Sarah O'Connor, then at the John Innes Centre, who systematically identified the missing enzymes through transcriptome mining of periwinkle cells.
By 2020, a complete pathway from precursor to vincristine had been reconstituted in yeast, in collaboration between O'Connor's group and others.
The yields are still very low compared to what would be needed for industrial production, but the demonstration that the pathway can be reconstituted at all is a milestone.
It is also a beautiful illustration of how modern transcriptome-based pathway discovery turned a thirty-year mystery into a project that could be completed in about a decade.
Opioids.
Morphine, codeine, thebaine, and the other opium alkaloids are biosynthesized in the opium poppy, Papaver somniferum, through a pathway involving more than twenty enzymes.

In 2015, Christina Smolke's lab at Stanford published a landmark paper demonstrating the first complete reconstitution of the opioid pathway in yeast — from glucose to thebaine, a precursor that can be converted chemically to morphine and codeine.
The Smolke project required identifying and assembling roughly twenty enzymes from the plant, engineering their activity in the yeast context, and balancing the cofactor and intermediate flows.
The yields, again, were initially very low — far below what would be commercially relevant — but the demonstration was startling.
The yeast made thebaine from sugar.
That had never been done before, and the moment the paper came out, it kicked off a public debate about the implications.
I want to spend a minute on that debate, because it captures something important about the social dimension of metabolic engineering.

The concern was straightforward: if anyone with a yeast culture and a fermenter can make opioids, then home production of controlled substances might become possible, potentially undermining the regulatory regime that controls medical opioids and their illegal counterparts.
Op-eds appeared.
Hearings were held.
Policy proposals were floated.

The reality is that the yields demonstrated in the 2015 paper were far too low for home production to be practical, and even at much improved yields the engineering and equipment requirements would be substantial.
But the broader point stands.

As metabolic engineering makes more chemicals more easily, more widely, with more accessible tools, the regulatory regime that controls drug production — built around the assumption that synthesis is hard and centralized — will face new challenges.
This is one of the genuine dual-use concerns in modern biotechnology, alongside concerns about bioweapons and gene drives, and the field is going to have to engage with it.
The analogy I want you to carry: opiates in yeast — Smolke's 2015 paper — was the wow moment for plant alkaloid metabolic engineering.
The yeast made thebaine from glucose, completing a twenty-enzyme pathway transplanted from poppy.
It opened the door to fermentative production of controlled substances and started a still-ongoing public debate about home-brewing.
Whatever you think of the policy implications, the scientific demonstration was a watershed for what could be done.

There are now active programs targeting many other plant natural products — cannabinoids (in yeast and microbes), psilocybin and related psychedelics (in yeast and E. coli), shikimate-derived alkaloids of various kinds, terpenoids by the dozens, glycosylated steroids, polyketides for antibiotics and immunosuppressants, and so on.
The list is growing every year.

The dominant pattern is: identify the plant pathway, reconstitute it in yeast, optimize for industrial yields, and either license to a pharmaceutical partner or build out fermentation capacity in-house.
Companies like Demetrix (psychedelics and other targets), Antheia (opioids and other alkaloids), Ginkgo's various partnerships, and many others operate in this space.
The plant natural product space is, alongside alt-protein, one of the most active commercial frontiers of metabolic engineering.
Now let us turn to a very different application — the sustainable manufacturing of bulk chemicals that today come from petroleum.
